title: Hierarchical Navigable Small World (HNSW) vs Inverted File (IVF) for Vector Search
excerpt: Comparison of HNSW and IVF algorithms for efficient vector search in high-dimensional spaces.

title: Why nn.Linear Stores Weights Transposed

source.title: Build a Large Language Model (From Scratch)
source.author: Sebastian Raschka

summary: Why PyTorch's nn.Linear stores weights as (d_out, d_in) instead of (d_in, d_out) — and how this transposition causes silent bugs when copying weights between nn.Parameter and nn.Linear.
excerpt: PyTorch stores nn.Linear weights as (d_out, d_in) — the transpose of what you'd intuitively expect. This is intentional: internally, nn.Linear computes x @ W.T + b. But it means if you ever try to move weights between a raw nn.Parameter and an nn.Linear, you'll get a shape mismatch — or worse, a silent wrong answer if the dimensions happen to be square.

title: Scaled Dot-Product Attention: Why we divide by √d_k

source.title: Build a Large Language Model (From Scratch)

summary: Why scaled dot-product attention divides scores by √d_k before softmax — and how this prevents vanishing gradients during training.
excerpt: The problem isn't that softmax outputs stop summing to 1. The problem is the shape of the distribution — large dot products cause softmax to collapse into a near-binary spike, killing gradients.

title: Scaled Dot-Product Attention

source.title: Build a Large Language Model (From Scratch)
source.author: Sebastian Raschka

summary: Comprehensive notes on scaled dot-product attention covering the formula, scaling intuition, causal masking approaches (mask before vs after softmax), and PyTorch implementation.
excerpt: The dot product QK^T measures similarity between query and key vectors — high dot product = high relevance. Scaling before softmax is critical for training stability.

title: Understanding Dropout in Attention Mechanisms

source.title: Build a Large Language Model (From Scratch)
source.author: Sebastian Raschka

summary: How dropout works in attention mechanisms — probabilistic zeroing, inverted dropout scaling, and the effect of different dropout rates on learning in transformers.
excerpt: Dropout is probabilistic, not deterministic. With p=0.5, each element independently has a 50% chance of being dropped — the actual count varies per run.

title: Multi-Head Attention

source.title: Build a Large Language Model (From Scratch)
source.author: Sebastian Raschka

summary: Comprehensive notes on multi-head attention covering the wrapper approach, efficient implementation with weight splitting, shape transformations through the forward pass, causal masking, and GPT-2 specifications.
excerpt: Multi-head attention allows each head to learn different aspects of the input simultaneously — syntax, semantics, coreference — producing rich context vectors through parallel weighted sums.
